summary: Julia Evans shares her appearance on the Ruby Rogues podcast, where she discussed systems programming tools like strace for debugging everyday code, building an OS in Rust, and the value of asking questions to learn. She expresses pleasant surprise at how the conversation ranged well beyond Ruby into topics like Rust, statistics, and strace. She notes the podcast referenced 13 of her blog posts, and reflects on the fun of cross-pollinating ideas across language communities.
key_points: Systems programming tools like strace are useful for debugging regular application code, not just low-level systems work
Asking questions you think are 'stupid' is a powerful learning strategy
Technical conversations benefit from crossing community boundaries — a Ruby podcast can productively discuss Rust, statistics, and Linux tools
Evans's prolific blogging had generated enough material that 13 posts were referenced in a single podcast episode
money_quotes: We talked about using systems programming tools (like strace) to debug your regular pedestrian code.
How asking stupid questions is an amazing way to learn.
It is totally okay to spend a Ruby podcast discussing Rust, statistics, strace, and, well… not Ruby :)
key_insight: Systems programming tools and a willingness to ask naive questions are valuable across all programming communities, not just in low-level work.
